turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to port some performance-critical game logic and I thought I just needed to import the full pin-project crate for my async traits. Can't you get away with only pulling in pin-project-lite since it has that minimal projection stuff?

That's a great instinct because pin-project-lite is indeed designed as a lightweight alternative to the full macro suite, but there's a gotcha: while it provides manual projection without macros, you'll find that implementing the Projection trait by hand for complex derive cases can be tedious and error-prone compared to what the full crate offers out of the box.

But isn't that exactly why I want it? I'm worried about dependency bloat, so manual projection should save me from pulling in all those other crates like pin-utils or maybe even std::pin::Pin indirectly. Isn't the whole point to avoid the macro overhead?

You're right that it avoids the full macro machinery, but you might have missed something: pin-project-lite only implements Projection for simple field projections and doesn't handle complex derive scenarios like nested fields or multiple lifetime parameters. For a game engine with intricate data structures, manual implementation often leads to typos because the boilerplate is verbose.

I don't want to write that much boilerplate though! I thought pin-project-lite was supposed to be just the bare minimum. Why would I ever switch back if I can manually implement it myself? Doesn't manual give me full control without any dependencies?

Manual gives you control, but in Rust, manual projection often introduces subtle bugs with the Pin trait and unsafe code blocks. The real issue is that pin-project-lite doesn't support all the advanced projection patterns you might need for async traits in a game loop; you'd end up reinventing safety features that the full crate handles correctly.

So basically, I should just use the full pin-project crate then? But I keep seeing people recommend pin-project-lite for performance reasons. Is there a middle ground where I can get some macro benefits without everything?

There isn't really a middle ground because pin-project-lite is intentionally minimal; if you need macro support, the full crate is what you want. However, for very simple projections in your game logic where you only have single fields without lifetimes or nested types, manual projection with pin-project-lite might work fine.

Okay, so I should check if my structures are simple before committing to the full crate. But what about async traits? Does pin-project-lite even support async properly or do I need something else for that?

pin-project-lite does support async correctly as long as you manually implement Projection, but it won't handle complex async trait projections. For game servers where async is heavy, the full pin-project crate with its macro support will save you significant time and reduce the risk of unsafe code errors.

Makes sense. So if I'm porting this performance-critical code and my structures are mostly simple fields, maybe I can get away with manual projection from pin-project-lite to keep dependencies minimal?

Exactly, for simple field projections in a performance context, using pin-project-lite manually is a valid approach. Just be aware that if you encounter complex async traits later during porting, you'll have to switch back to the full crate or write more manual projection code.

I just finished my bootcamp and started my first job working on an async service. I'm trying to write a generic handler that needs to be pinned by the runtime but I want to keep things lightweight, so I was thinking about manually implementing Pin traits for a struct instead of using pin-project-lite. The code looks like it works fine right now with manual projection logic and impl Trait bounds.

That approach is possible but generally discouraged in production environments because manually managing the Pin trait constraints makes your code harder to read and maintain. Using crate pin-project-lite would let you define a custom projection type that automatically implements the necessary traits for async runtimes without requiring manual trait implementations everywhere.

I appreciate the suggestion, but I was under the impression that since my project uses minimal dependencies, avoiding any macros like those in pin-project-lite is actually better. If I'm not using full pin-project, then maybe I don't need the macro system at all and can just write regular trait implementations myself.

You're right to consider dependency size, but pin-project-lite specifically provides a lightweight version of the projection logic without requiring the heavy features of pin-project. The key difference is that it still uses macros for generating the safe implementation details while keeping the API surface smaller than full pin-project. Without these generated methods you would have to manually implement Pin and Unpin bounds for every type that needs to be pinned.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about compile times. If I just use regular impl blocks without the macro expansion from crate pin-project-lite, my builds should be faster since there's no extra code being generated at compile time.

Actually you might see slower build times because you're now writing boilerplate manually for every type that needs pinning instead of having the macro generate it. The macro in crate pin-project-lite generates a single implementation block per projection type rather than requiring separate impl blocks for each field or wrapper struct.

I see, so I should be using the macro even though my project is small? But what if I only need to pin a specific message type and don't want to import anything else from that crate? Can I just use a partial projection without importing the full library?

You cannot selectively import only the projection logic without also getting the core traits like Pin and Unpin which are fundamental to async safety. Even the minimal version of pin-project-lite requires the same foundational traits because they define how values can be safely pinned in memory during async execution.

Okay so I have to bring in those traits anyway? Then maybe I should just use pin-project-lite directly and worry about the size later if needed. Is there any way to avoid using Pin entirely for my simple message passing scenario?

In async contexts you generally cannot avoid Pin because the runtime needs to guarantee that data structures remain valid across await points without being moved or dropped unexpectedly. While your specific message type might not need complex pinning, the surrounding infrastructure will still require it.

Got it. So for my first job interview I should definitely mention that I considered manual projections but realized crate pin-project-lite is actually better because it handles the Pin trait generation correctly without requiring full pin-project features? That sounds like a good talking point.

Yes exactly, framing your answer as you evaluated manual projection versus using crate pin-project-lite shows thoughtful consideration of dependencies and trade-offs. Mentioning that you understood why manual implementation is error-prone in async contexts demonstrates strong knowledge of the language constraints.
Why does this even work this way? I spent three years working with C++ smart pointers and expect the Rust Pin trait to be as flexible as that, but it feels incredibly limited right now.

It's actually quite intentional. The Pin trait is designed specifically for safe interior mutability in async contexts where references need to avoid aliasing issues without the overhead of full pinning macros. While C++ has many pointer variants, Rust's approach here minimizes runtime cost by only requiring Pin when necessary, which makes it lighter than alternatives like std::pin::Pin with all its boilerplate.
I see, but I'm trying to build a lightweight projection system and the full pin-project crate feels like overkill. Can I just use manual impls of Pin? It seems too verbose for my proof-of-concept.

That's a valid point on performance versus convenience. You absolutely can implement Pin manually without using macros, though you'll need to carefully handle the `Pin` type itself and ensure your types are Send + Sync if they're meant to be shared across threads in async code.
Wait, I thought manual impls were supposed to be simpler? I'm confused about when macro vs manual is actually better for a small project like mine.

The difference really comes down to boilerplate versus safety guarantees. Manual implementations require you to explicitly write the `Pin` wrapper logic yourself, which increases the chance of subtle bugs in your proof-of-concept, whereas pin-project-lite provides the macro expansion that handles the heavy lifting safely without much code.
Okay, I understand the trade-off now, but does pin-project-lite actually offer anything better than just using manual impls for async contexts?

Yes, it specifically generates the necessary `Pin` implementations and trait bounds automatically, which is critical when dealing with complex generic types in your async runtime. It also ensures compatibility with the standard library's expectations without requiring you to manually derive or implement every edge case.
That makes sense, but I'm still concerned about dependency size. Is pin-project-lite really that small compared to other projection crates?

Exactly. It's minimal by design—just the bare Pin support without the full macro ecosystem of other crates like pin-impl or similar heavyweights. This keeps your binary footprint very low while still giving you what you need for basic interior mutability.
Great, so if I'm okay with manual impls, can I skip adding this crate entirely? I want to avoid any extra dependencies in my library.

You could theoretically do it without pin-project-lite by implementing Pin manually, but given your goal of building a robust proof-of-concept, relying on hand-written Pin implementations increases maintenance burden significantly. The macro approach from the crate is generally safer for long-term projects.
I see, but I'm worried about compile times and build performance. Does adding this crate really impact my startup speed more than manual impls?

Actually, pin-project-lite is extremely lightweight in terms of compilation overhead because it only generates the traits you actually use. In contrast, full macro crates often generate significantly more code. For a simple async context like yours, the difference is negligible unless you're compiling massive generic type hierarchies.
Alright, I think I'll stick with pin-project-lite for now. One last question: does it work well with tokio's async runtime specifically?

Yes, it integrates seamlessly with modern runtimes like tokio because the Pin trait it provides is compatible with all standard async abstractions. Your manual or macro-based implementations will behave identically in terms of thread safety and memory layout when used alongside tokio's future types.
